units.name: Unit 1: Primitive Types
units.objectives:
UNIT 1: Primitive Types — AP CSA CED Learning Objectives

VAR-1.A: Identify the most appropriate data type category for a particular specification.
- Primitive types in Java: int (integer, −2³¹ to 2³¹−1), double (floating-point), boolean (true/false).
- String is a reference type, not primitive.

VAR-1.B: Declare variables of the correct types to represent simple data.
- Syntax: type name = value; Example: int score = 95;

CON-1.B: Evaluate arithmetic expressions in a program code.
- Operators: +, −, *, /, % (modulo). Integer division truncates: 7/2 = 3. Double division: 7.0/2 = 3.5.
- Operator precedence: * / % before + −. Left-to-right for same precedence.

CON-1.C: Evaluate what is stored in a variable as a result of an expression with an assignment statement.
- Compound assignment: +=, −=, *=, /=, %=. Increment/decrement: ++, −− (pre and post).

CON-1.D: Evaluate arithmetic expressions that use casting.
- Casting: (int)3.9 = 3 (truncates). (double)7/2 = 3.5. Widening (int→double) automatic; narrowing (double→int) requires explicit cast.
- Overflow: int has fixed range; exceeding it causes unexpected results.

VAR-1.D: Identify the value of a variable using its scope when the variable is accessed.
- Scope: variable accessible only within the block it was declared.

units.name: Unit 2: Using Objects
decks: Calling a Void Method with Parameters
String Objects — Concatenation, Literals, and More
units.objectives:
UNIT 2: Using Objects — AP CSA CED Learning Objectives

VAR-1.E: Describe the relationship between a class and an object.
- Class = blueprint/template. Object = instance of a class. Objects have state (instance variables) and behavior (methods).

VAR-1.F: Create an object by calling a constructor with parameters.
- Syntax: ClassName varName = new ClassName(params);
- Constructor initializes the object's state.

CON-1.E: Call methods on objects.
- Void methods: perform action, return nothing. Syntax: object.method(args);
- Non-void methods: return a value. Must use the return value.

VAR-1.G: Explain the relationship between an object reference and the object it refers to.
- Reference variable stores a memory address. null means no object referenced. Multiple references can point to same object.

CON-2.A: Represent string values.
- String literals: "hello". Concatenation (+): "Hello" + " " + "World" = "Hello World".
- String immutability: methods return new strings, do not modify original.

CON-2.B: Call String methods.
- length(), substring(start), substring(start, end), indexOf(str), equals(str), compareTo(str).
- substring(0, 3) returns chars at index 0, 1, 2. Index is 0-based.

CON-2.D: Use the Math class.
- Math.abs(x), Math.pow(base, exp), Math.sqrt(x), Math.random() [0.0, 1.0), Math.max(a,b), Math.min(a,b).
- All Math methods are static — called on the class, not an instance.

VAR-1.C: Use wrapper classes.
- Integer.MIN_VALUE, Integer.MAX_VALUE. Autoboxing/unboxing: auto conversion between int and Integer.

units.name: Unit 3: Boolean Expressions and if Statements
units.objectives:
UNIT 3: Boolean Expressions and if Statements — AP CSA CED Learning Objectives

CON-3.A: Evaluate Boolean expressions that use relational operators.
- Relational: ==, !=, <, >, <=, >=. Return boolean.
- For primitives, == compares values. For objects, == compares references.

CON-3.B: Represent branching logical processes by using conditional statements.
- if(condition){ ... }
- if(condition){ ... } else { ... }
- if(cond1){ ... } else if(cond2){ ... } else { ... }
- Only one branch executes. Nested if-else.

CON-3.C: Evaluate compound Boolean expressions.
- Logical AND: &&. Logical OR: ||. Logical NOT: !.
- Short-circuit evaluation: && stops if left is false; || stops if left is true.
- De Morgan's Laws: !(A && B) == (!A || !B); !(A || B) == (!A && !B).

CON-3.D: Represent equivalent Boolean expressions.
- Equivalent forms using De Morgan's and distributive laws. Truth tables.

CON-3.E: Compare object references using Boolean expressions.
- Use .equals() to compare String content, not ==.
- null check: obj != null before calling methods to avoid NullPointerException.

units.name: Unit 4: Iteration
units.objectives:
UNIT 4: Iteration — AP CSA CED Learning Objectives

CON-4.A: Represent iterative processes using a while loop.
- while(condition){ body } — evaluates condition before each iteration. Off-by-one errors.
- do-while: body executes at least once (not on exam but understand concept).
- Infinite loop if condition never becomes false. Loop variable must update.

CON-4.B: Represent iterative processes using a for loop.
- for(init; condition; update){ body }. Equivalent to while loop.
- Loop variable scope: only accessible inside loop body (unless declared outside).
- Common pattern: for(int i = 0; i < n; i++) iterates n times.

CON-4.C: Trace the execution of iterative statements.
- Trace table: track variable values after each iteration.
- Common algorithms: sum of array, find min/max, count occurrences, build string.

CON-4.D: Represent nested iterative processes.
- Nested loops: inner loop runs completely for each outer loop iteration.
- Total iterations = outer × inner (when independent). Used for 2D arrays, patterns.

CON-4.E: Compute informal run-time analysis.
- Loops that run n times: O(n). Nested loops: O(n²). Understand how iteration count scales with input size.

units.name: Unit 5: Writing Classes
decks: Ethical and Social Implications of Computing
units.objectives:
UNIT 5: Writing Classes — AP CSA CED Learning Objectives

MOD-2.A: Describe the attributes (data) and behaviors (methods) of an object.
- Instance variables declared at class level. Methods define behavior.
- Encapsulation: private instance variables, public accessor/mutator methods.

MOD-2.B: Describe the purpose and use of constructors.
- Constructor has same name as class, no return type. Initializes instance variables.
- Default constructor (no params). Overloading: multiple constructors with different params.
- If no constructor is written, Java provides a no-arg default.

MOD-2.C: Describe the purpose of accessor (get) and mutator (set) methods.
- Accessor (getter): returns value of instance variable. Non-void. Does not modify state.
- Mutator (setter): modifies instance variable. Void. Takes parameter.

MOD-2.E: Explain static class and instance variables and methods.
- static variables: shared by all instances. static methods: belong to class, not instance, cannot access instance variables. Math.random() is static.
- Instance variables: each object has its own copy.

VAR-1.H: Explain the relationship between a subclass and a superclass using the keyword this.
- this.varName distinguishes instance variable from local variable with same name in constructor/method.

IOC-1.A: Explain the ethical and social implications of computer programs.
- Privacy, security, intellectual property, access, effects on society.

units.name: Unit 6: Array
units.objectives:
UNIT 6: Array — AP CSA CED Learning Objectives

VAR-2.A: Represent collections of related primitive or object reference data using one-dimensional array objects.
- Declaration: int[] arr = new int[n]; — creates array of n elements, default values (0 for int, null for objects, false for boolean).
- Initialization: int[] arr = {1, 2, 3, 4};
- Index: 0-based. arr[0] to arr[arr.length − 1]. ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException if out of range.

VAR-2.B: Traverse a one-dimensional array.
- Standard for loop: for(int i = 0; i < arr.length; i++). Access arr[i].
- Enhanced for loop: for(int val : arr) — read only, cannot modify array elements or know index.

CON-2.J: Implement standard algorithms involving arrays.
- Traversal algorithms: find min/max, sum, average, count.
- Sequential (linear) search: O(n), compare each element. Returns index or −1.
- Selection sort: repeatedly find minimum and swap into place. O(n²).
- Insertion sort: build sorted portion one element at a time. O(n²).
- Note: Arrays.sort() not expected to be implemented, but result of sorting must be understood.

units.name: Unit 7: ArrayList
units.objectives:
UNIT 7: ArrayList — AP CSA CED Learning Objectives

VAR-2.C: Represent collections of related object reference data using ArrayList objects.
- Import: import java.util.ArrayList;
- Declaration: ArrayList<Type> list = new ArrayList<Type>();
- ArrayList stores objects only — use Integer, Double, String (not int, double).
- Dynamic size: grows/shrinks as elements are added/removed.

VAR-2.D: Traverse an ArrayList.
- Methods: add(obj), add(index, obj), get(index), set(index, obj), remove(index), size().
- Standard for loop with get(i). Enhanced for loop. Avoid modifying ArrayList while traversing with enhanced for (ConcurrentModificationException risk).

CON-2.K: Implement standard ArrayList algorithms.
- Search: linear search using get(i) in a loop. Remove during traversal: traverse backwards, or use standard for loop decrementing index after remove.
- Sort: Collections.sort() or hand-written selection/insertion sort using ArrayList methods.

IOC-1.B: Explain the risks of collecting and storing personal data.
- Data collection, privacy, identity theft. Ethical responsibilities of programmers.

units.name: Unit 8: 2D Array
units.objectives:
UNIT 8: 2D Array — AP CSA CED Learning Objectives

VAR-2.E: Represent collections of related primitive or object reference data using two-dimensional array objects.
- Declaration: int[][] matrix = new int[rows][cols];
- Initialization: int[][] grid = {{1,2,3},{4,5,6}};
- Access: matrix[row][col]. Row-major order. matrix.length = number of rows. matrix[0].length = number of cols.
- Default values same as 1D arrays.

VAR-2.F: Traverse a two-dimensional array.
- Nested for loops: outer iterates rows (i from 0 to matrix.length−1), inner iterates columns (j from 0 to matrix[i].length−1).
- Row-major vs. column-major traversal. Enhanced for: for(int[] row : matrix) then for(int val : row).
- Algorithms on 2D arrays: find min/max, sum of row/column, count occurrences, check diagonal.
- Ragged arrays: rows can have different lengths (matrix[i].length may differ by row).

units.name: Unit 9: Inheritance
units.objectives:
UNIT 9: Inheritance — AP CSA CED Learning Objectives

MOD-3.A: Describe the relationship between a superclass and a subclass.
- extends keyword: public class Dog extends Animal. Subclass inherits all public/protected members.
- IS-A relationship. Subclass can add new methods/variables and override inherited methods.

MOD-3.B: Create constructors in a subclass.
- super(params) must be first statement in subclass constructor to call superclass constructor.
- If super() not called explicitly, Java calls no-arg superclass constructor (compile error if none exists).

MOD-3.C: Override methods.
- @Override annotation. Method signature (name + params) must match exactly.
- Overriding vs. overloading: overloading = same name, different params (not inheritance-specific).

MOD-3.D: Use the super keyword to call a superclass method.
- super.methodName() explicitly calls superclass version from overriding method.

MOD-3.E: Explain polymorphism.
- Polymorphism: a reference variable of type Superclass can hold an object of Subclass type.
- Animal a = new Dog(); — at runtime, a.sound() calls Dog's overriding version. Dynamic dispatch.
- Cannot call subclass-only methods via superclass reference without casting.

MOD-3.F: Describe the Object class and its methods.
- All classes extend Object. Object methods: toString(), equals(Object o).
- Override toString() to provide meaningful String representation. Override equals() to compare by content.

units.name: Unit 10: Recursion
units.objectives:
UNIT 10: Recursion — AP CSA CED Learning Objectives

CON-2.L: Evaluate recursive programs.
- A recursive method calls itself. Must have: base case (terminates recursion) and recursive case (reduces toward base case).
- Call stack: each recursive call adds a stack frame. Stack overflow if base case never reached.
- Trace recursive execution by substituting values.

CON-2.O: Determine the result of executing recursive programs.
- Factorial: n! = n × (n−1)! with base case 0! = 1.
- Fibonacci: fib(n) = fib(n−1) + fib(n−2) with base cases fib(0)=0, fib(1)=1.
- String recursion: reverse, palindrome check, substring search.

CON-2.P: Evaluate Binary Search and Merge Sort algorithms.
- Binary search (recursive): O(log n). Array must be sorted. Compare target to middle element; recurse on left or right half.
- Merge sort (recursive): O(n log n). Divide array in half; recursively sort each half; merge.
- Understand the idea of divide-and-conquer; can compare efficiency to iterative O(n) search and O(n²) sorts.
